title: Dog Green: The Longest Morning
description: June 6, 1944, 0630 hours. The ramps of the landing craft are dropping on the deadliest stretch of Omaha Beach. Three men — a coxswain, a Ranger sergeant, and a battalion medic — must navigate chaos, impossible choices, and the rising tide to break through the Atlantic Wall at the Vierville Draw before the second wave drowns in the same blood as the first.

pressureEvents: A mortar round detonates thirty yards to the left — the concussion knocks men flat and the tide advances another six inches up the sand.
MG-42 enfilade fire from WN-71 sweeps the waterline; men caught in the open go down in clusters and the beach narrows as the tide rises.
Radio contact with USS McCook cuts to static — the destroyer's fire support is blind until someone on shore marks the bunker positions.
The second wave of LCAs is visible on the horizon — they will hit the same obstacles in twelve minutes unless the draw is opened.
A wounded man in the surf grabs at a passing soldier's ankle, screaming; every second spent there is a second the wire remains uncut.

winConditions: The Bangalore Torpedo is detonated and the concertina wire at the base of the Vierville Draw is breached.
Survivors reach the top of the bluffs at WN-71 before 0800 hours and signal the USS McCook.
The draw is opened in time for the second wave's armor to pass through before the tide closes the beach.
failConditions: All three player characters are killed or incapacitated before reaching the Vierville Draw.
The session clock expires at 0800 hours with the wire unbreached and the draw still under German control.
The Bangalore Torpedo is destroyed by enemy fire before it can be placed.
partialSuccessExamples: The wire is blown and the draw opens, but the Ranger sergeant does not survive to see it — his story ends at the shingle.
The coxswain beaches the craft successfully and the men land dry, but the medic is lost in the surf triage and the assault pushes inland without him.
The bluffs are taken but the draw remains blocked by a disabled tank; the second wave must fight through the surf as the first wave did.

sections.text: It is the morning of June 6, 1944, and the largest seaborne invasion in human history is already fracturing along its most critical seam. Operation Overlord has committed 156,000 Allied soldiers to five beaches along a fifty-mile stretch of the Normandy coast, but at Dog Green Sector — the westernmost sub-sector of Omaha Beach, fronting the Vierville-sur-Mer draw — the German 352nd Infantry Division has placed its most experienced machine-gun crews in positions that command every yard of open sand. The men of the 116th Infantry Regiment and the 2nd Ranger Battalion do not yet know that the aerial bombardment missed the bluffs by three miles, that the amphibious DD tanks have sunk in the Channel swells, and that they will cross five hundred yards of killing ground with no armor and no air cover. The Atlantic Wall here is not a wall — it is a carefully engineered abattoir, and the tide is rising.

If the Vierville Draw — Exit D-1 — cannot be opened by mid-morning, the entire western flank of Omaha Beach remains a closed pocket, and the armor massing offshore has nowhere to land. Supreme Commander Eisenhower has already drafted a message conceding failure; it sits in his breast pocket. For the men in the water right now, the stakes are not strategic — they are the width of a rifle barrel, the distance between a steel obstacle and a man's chest, the difference between a morphine syrette reaching a wounded soldier before the tide does. History will record what happens at the macro scale; the men making that history have no access to that record. They have only the next ten yards of sand.

The air at 0630 hours smells of cordite, diesel exhaust, and salt water gone wrong — a mineral, copper-edged smell that the veterans will later struggle to name. The English Channel is running a four-foot chop and the sky is a low, grey ceiling of overcast that turns the beach into a colorless panorama of smoke and spray. Steel hedgehogs and Belgian Gates crowd the surf line, many of them tipped with Teller mines that detonate against the hulls of landing craft with a flat, percussive crack. The shingle — a bank of smooth fist-sized stones above the waterline — is the only cover on the beach, and it is two hundred yards from the water's edge. Between the surf and the shingle, the sand is open, flat, and entirely without mercy.

You are one of three men whose choices in the next ninety minutes will determine whether any part of this unit survives to climb the bluffs at Vierville. The coxswain is still at the tiller, reading the surf for a gap between the mines. The Ranger sergeant is crouched behind a hedgehog with a Bangalore Torpedo across his knees and the wire fifty yards ahead of him. The medic is on his hands and knees in the surf, working on a man who may already be past saving, while three others call for him from the shingle. The ramp of the nearest LCA is about to drop. You are standing at the exact moment where individual decisions stop being personal and become, without anyone's permission, historical.

character_entries.role_coxswain: Sixty yards out, and the hull is telling you everything the armored glass cannot — a low, grinding shudder through the tiller that means the surf is steepening over a sandbar, a rhythm the engine has no part in. The men behind you are a single mass of wool and webbing and held breath, and you have not looked at them since the last near-miss blew spray across the cockpit and left your ears ringing with a frequency the engine cannot touch. What you know sits in your chest like swallowed iron: the direct line is mined, the course correction you made two minutes ago will put them into chest-deep water instead of firm sand, and the radio crackling behind your left shoulder is the only voice on this beach that can reach the McCook's five-inch guns. The tidal window is forty minutes, maybe thirty-eight now. There is a gap in the Belgian Gate line thirty yards west — you can see it, the way you can read a current from the way light breaks on moving water — and no one has asked you, and you have not said. The ramp lock is vibrating under the surf pressure. Your hand is on the tiller. The next thing you do will be the thing you live with.
character_entries.role_ranger_sergeant: The Bangalore section is across your knees and the water is at your waist and you have been counting. Six minutes behind this hedgehog. Forty-three seconds between MG-42 bursts at the primary arc, eleven seconds when it walks left, and on the third traverse you saw it — the dead zone, fifteen yards left of the wire, a corridor of maybe four feet where the gun cannot go without the emplacement rotating, which it has not done once. You know this. Hargrove does not know this. Hargrove is at the shingle twenty yards behind you shouting assembly and you can hear him the way you can hear the surf, as background, as weather, because what you are doing is arithmetic and the arithmetic is not finished. One Bangalore section gives you an unreliable breach, a partial cut, a gap that may not pass a man sideways. Two sections give you a lane. Drennan has the second section and Drennan went into the surf somewhere east of you and has not come up, and the tide is still rising, and the wire is still fifty yards across open sand that is not sand anymore but a geometry of death you have memorized down to the yard markers. The frontal push Hargrove is assembling will walk men directly across the primary arc. You have not moved to join it. The water reaches your chest now, cold enough to make the arithmetic feel like the only warm thing left, and somewhere behind you Hargrove is shouting your name, and you are looking east into the surf for Drennan, and you are looking left at the dead zone, and you take one breath.
character_entries.role_battalion_medic: Your knees are on sand that is no longer sand — it is a suspension of salt water and something darker, and the four-foot chop is at your elbows now, which means it was at your wrists three minutes ago, which means the arithmetic is not in your favor. The man beneath your hands presents with a traumatic amputation at the mid-femur, the stump already grey-white in the cold water, the hemorrhage rate suggesting he has perhaps eight minutes of useful pressure left before the decision is made for you by his cardiovascular system rather than by you. You have sutured the clinical language around yourself like a dressing — *presents with, suggests, indicates* — because the clinical language is the only thing standing between you and the fact that you know his name, that he told you about a sister in Roanoke while you were still in the Channel, that he is twenty-two years old and looking at the sky with an expression that is not fear but something quieter and more final than fear. Thirty feet to your left, the soldier you marked expectant — *penetrating abdominal trauma, non-survivable, do not expend resources* — is propped against a steel hedgehog and speaking with a precision that cuts through the surf noise in a way that screaming cannot. The light has shifted. The blood is wrong. It is running from his hairline, not his belly, and the man you wrote off twenty minutes ago is alive and mobile and saying something in careful, deliberate English that is not his first language, to no one, or to everyone, or to whoever will come. You know something the Ranger Sergeant does not know, something the Coxswain cannot know — you know that your triage assessment on Private Szymanski was wrong, that the error is yours, that he is carrying words in his mouth that may matter more than anything in your bag, and that you cannot reach him until you finish what is under your hands or choose not to finish it. The morphine syrette is in your fingers. The tide is at your forearms. You look down at the man who told you about Roanoke, and then you look left at the man who should be dead and isn't, and the clinical language goes very quiet, and you take a breath.

categories.name: Royal Navy / LCVP Coxswain Argot
categories.context: Used by British Royal Navy and U.S. Navy coxswains piloting landing craft during the Normandy assault; spoken over engine noise and shellfire.

terms.meaning: The water between the transport ships and the beach, now churned by propellers, surf, and shellfire
Beaching the craft intentionally — dropping the ramp on dry or shallow sand rather than deep water
When a craft turns broadside to the surf and is swamped or capsized — a coxswain's worst fear
Using a stern anchor to pull a grounded craft back off the beach after unloading
The final approach from the line of departure to the beach — typically the most dangerous three minutes of the operation
Landing Craft Assault — the British-built armored craft carrying approximately 35 soldiers
A sandbar or shallow ridge that could ground a craft prematurely, leaving troops to wade in deep water under fire

categories.name: Ranger and Infantry Field Language
categories.context: Used by soldiers of the 116th Infantry and 2nd Ranger Battalion during combat operations; a mix of Army regulation terminology and front-line slang developed in training and North Africa.

terms.meaning: The bank of smooth stones above the waterline at Omaha Beach — the first real cover on the beach
Short form of Bangalore Torpedo — a sectional pipe explosive used to breach wire obstacles
Widerstandsnest — German 'resistance nest'; a fortified strongpoint. WN-71 and WN-72 bracketed the Vierville Draw
Gunfire directed along the length of a line of men rather than at its face — the most lethal geometry of fire on the beach
A natural valley or gully cutting through the bluffs — the only vehicle exit from the beach; controlling it is the day's objective
A small metal clicker used for friend-or-foe identification in low visibility; one click answered by two
The inflatable life belt worn over the combat jacket — named for its silhouette when inflated
A compact individual daily ration in a waxed cardboard box — carried but rarely eaten on D-Day

categories.name: Combat Medic and Triage Vocabulary
categories.context: Used by battalion aid men (medics) of the U.S. Army Medical Corps during field triage operations; drawn from both medical training and improvised practice under fire.

terms.meaning: Triage category for a wounded man whose injuries are fatal even with treatment — bypassed to conserve supplies for survivable cases
A single-dose morphine injector resembling a small toothpaste tube with a needle; the primary pain management tool in the field
Sulfonamide powder poured directly into a wound to prevent infection — every soldier carried a packet in his first-aid kit
Dried blood plasma reconstituted with sterile water and administered intravenously to treat shock from blood loss
A temporary forward medical post, often no more than a sheltered depression in the ground or the lee side of a seawall
Casualties mobile enough to move under their own power — lower priority for the medic, directed to move to the rear independently
The Geneva Convention marker on a medic's helmet and brassard — theoretically granting non-combatant protection, widely ignored at close range

categories.name: German Fortification and Fire Control Terms
categories.context: Terms used in German Army doctrine for the Atlantic Wall positions; known to Allied intelligence officers and translated into planning documents studied by Ranger NCOs before the assault.

terms.meaning: A small, circular, reinforced concrete pit for a single machine gun or mortar crew — low profile, difficult to spot from the beach
An anti-tank gun emplacement oriented to fire along the beach rather than out to sea — the source of much of the flanking fire
Security troops — the lower-grade garrison soldiers of the 716th Division, distinct from the combat-hardened 352nd Infantry
The German belt-fed machine gun firing at 1,200 rounds per minute — its distinctive tearing sound gave it the nickname 'Hitler's buzzsaw'
A large anti-tank pressure mine mounted atop beach obstacles to detonate against landing craft hulls

character_fates.name: Corporal James Drennan
character_fates.historical_record: No individual named James Drennan appears in the verified casualty rolls or after-action reports of the 2nd Ranger Battalion for June 6, 1944. The 2nd Ranger Battalion suffered approximately 50 percent casualties on D-Day across its companies. This character is a composite fictional figure placed within a documented unit. Outcome cannot be assigned from the historical record.

character_fates.name: Feldwebel Heinrich Brandt
character_fates.historical_record: No individual named Heinrich Brandt appears in surviving German personnel records for Widerstandsnest 71 or the 352nd Infantry Division for June 6, 1944. WN-71 was overrun by American forces during the morning of June 6, 1944, and its garrison was killed, captured, or withdrew. The specific fates of individual enlisted MG section commanders at WN-71 are not documented in surviving records. This character is a composite fictional figure placed within a documented position.

character_fates.name: First Lieutenant Calvin Hargrove
character_fates.historical_record: No individual named Calvin Hargrove appears in the verified officer roster or casualty records of Company A, 116th Infantry Regiment, 29th Infantry Division for June 6, 1944. Company A, 116th Infantry, suffered catastrophic casualties at Dog Green Sector on the morning of June 6, 1944, with the company effectively destroyed as a fighting unit within the first thirty minutes of landing. This character is a composite fictional figure placed within a documented unit.

character_fates.name: T/5 Raymond Calloway
character_fates.historical_record: No individual named Raymond Calloway appears in the verified enlisted records or casualty rolls of the 116th Infantry Regiment's medical personnel for June 6, 1944. This character is a composite fictional figure. The documented experience of battalion aid men at Dog Green Sector on June 6, 1944, is recorded in the 29th Infantry Division's after-action reports and in survivor testimony collected by the U.S. Army Center of Military History.

character_fates.name: Petty Officer Second Class Arthur Reed
character_fates.historical_record: No individual named Arthur Reed appears in the verified crew records of the 551st LCA Flotilla for June 6, 1944. Royal Navy LCA coxswains operating at Omaha Beach on June 6, 1944, suffered significant casualties. Individual crew fates for the 551st LCA Flotilla are not fully documented in publicly available records. This character is a composite fictional figure placed within a documented flotilla designation.

character_fates.name: Private Olek Szymanski
character_fates.historical_record: No individual named Olek Szymanski appears in the verified enlisted casualty rolls or morning reports of Company A, 116th Infantry Regiment for June 6, 1944. This character is a composite fictional figure placed within a documented unit. Company A, 116th Infantry, sustained casualties estimated at over 90 percent killed, wounded, or missing in the first assault wave at Dog Green Sector.

character_fates.name: Staff Sergeant Thomas Kowalski
character_fates.historical_record: No individual named Thomas Kowalski appears in the verified NCO roster or casualty records of Dog Company, 2nd Ranger Battalion for June 6, 1944. This character is a composite fictional figure placed within a documented unit. Dog Company, 2nd Ranger Battalion, landed at Omaha Beach on June 6, 1944, and participated in the assault on the Vierville draw sector.

immediate_outcome.summary: The Vierville draw — Exit D-1 — was not opened until approximately 0900 hours on June 6, 1944, after sustained small-unit actions by surviving elements of the 116th Infantry Regiment and Ranger units, combined with direct naval gunfire support from destroyers including USS McCook and USS Carmick, which closed to within approximately 800 yards of the beach to engage German strongpoints. Company A of the 116th Infantry Regiment, which landed in the first wave at Dog Green Sector, was effectively destroyed as a fighting unit within the first thirty minutes, suffering casualties estimated at over 90 percent killed, wounded, or missing.
key_facts: Company A, 116th Infantry Regiment, 29th Infantry Division, landed at Dog Green Sector at approximately 0636 hours on June 6, 1944.

The 741st Tank Battalion's DD tanks, assigned to support the Omaha Beach assault, lost 27 of 29 tanks launched in the eastern sector; the 743rd Tank Battalion, assigned to the western sector including Dog Green, launched tanks directly from LCTs closer to shore, with better survival rates.

The 352nd Infantry Division, a full-strength experienced German division, had moved into the Omaha Beach defensive sector in March 1944; Allied intelligence had not confirmed its presence at the time of the assault.

The USAAF bombardment preceding the landing at Omaha Beach dropped approximately 13,000 bombs; due to cloud cover and concern about hitting landing craft, bombardiers delayed release, causing bombs to fall up to three miles inland, leaving German beach defenses largely intact.
USS McCook (DD-496) and USS Carmick (DD-493) provided direct fire support against WN-71 and adjacent strongpoints during the morning of June 6, 1944, closing to ranges of approximately 800 yards.
Exit D-1 at Vierville-sur-Mer was reported opened to vehicle traffic by approximately 1100 hours on June 6, 1944.
The 2nd Ranger Battalion landed elements at Dog Green and adjacent sectors on June 6, 1944, with Dog Company among the units committed to the Vierville draw area.

historical_frame: Omaha Beach was the only Allied landing beach on June 6, 1944, where the outcome was in serious doubt during the assault; the other four beaches — Utah, Gold, Juno, and Sword — achieved their objectives with significantly lower casualties.

American casualties at Omaha Beach on June 6, 1944, are estimated at approximately 2,000 killed, wounded, and missing, out of a total Allied casualty figure for D-Day estimated between 10,000 and 12,000 across all beaches and airborne operations.

The five beach exits at Omaha Beach — designated D-1 through F-1 — were the only vehicle routes off the beach; the bluffs flanking them rose to approximately 150 feet and were impassable to wheeled and tracked vehicles except through the draws.

open_threads.historical_record: The navigation decisions made by individual LCA coxswains at Omaha Beach on June 6, 1944, were not systematically documented in after-action reports. The Royal Navy's official report on LCA operations at Omaha noted that strong tidal drift of approximately 1 knot to the east caused many craft to land off their designated sectors. Whether individual coxswains chose to navigate through or around the obstacle belt, and the reasoning behind those choices, is not recorded for specific craft. The U.S. Army's official history notes that the obstacle belt extended from approximately 250 yards offshore at low tide and that many craft were holed or destroyed by Teller mines and Element C obstacles. The precise navigational choices made at the craft level remain undocumented.

The movement of individual Rangers from disabled or sunk craft to the shingle at Dog Green Sector is documented only in aggregate in after-action reports and in selected survivor testimony. The 2nd Ranger Battalion's official after-action report for June 6, 1944, describes the general conditions of the landing but does not reconstruct the movement of individual enlisted men across the beach. The specific paths taken by Rangers who entered the water from disabled craft and reached the shingle are not documented at the individual level in surviving primary sources.

The triage decisions made by individual aid men in the surf and on the beach at Dog Green Sector on June 6, 1944, are not documented in surviving medical records at the individual decision level. The 29th Infantry Division's medical after-action report describes the general collapse of organized medical support in the first wave at Omaha Beach and notes that many wounded men drowned in the rising tide before they could be evacuated. The moral and practical triage choices made by individual T/5 aid men in the water are recorded only in fragmentary survivor testimony and are not part of any official inquiry record.

The coordination between surviving elements of the 116th Infantry Regiment and 2nd Ranger Battalion at the shingle below the Vierville draw on the morning of June 6, 1944, is documented only in general terms in after-action reports. The specific moment at which surviving soldiers from different units recognized the wire obstacle at the draw's base as the shared tactical problem is not recorded. The 116th Infantry Regiment's after-action report notes that wire obstacles at the draw exits delayed the advance but does not reconstruct the individual-level awareness or communication that preceded organized action against those obstacles.

The process by which naval gunfire was directed against WN-71 and adjacent strongpoints on June 6, 1944, is partially documented. USS McCook's deck log and action report for June 6, 1944, record that the ship engaged targets on the beach at Omaha in response to requests and observed fire, but the specific mechanism by which firing arc and position data for WN-71 was communicated from shore to ship is not fully reconstructed in surviving records. Naval gunfire liaison officers were assigned to the assault waves, but their individual actions and the specific intelligence they transmitted are not documented at the level of individual communications. The question of exactly how and when WN-71's position was fixed for naval gunfire purposes remains incompletely answered in the historical record.

The tactical decisions made by surviving junior officers of Company A, 116th Infantry Regiment, at Dog Green Sector on June 6, 1944, are largely undocumented because so few officers survived the first thirty minutes of the assault. The company's commanding officer, Captain Taylor Fellers, was killed in the first minutes of the landing; the fates and decisions of his surviving lieutenants are not fully reconstructed in after-action reports. The U.S. Army's official history, 'Cross-Channel Attack' by Gordon Harrison (1951), notes that organized assault on the Vierville draw was eventually achieved by mixed groups of survivors acting largely on individual initiative after the command structure of the first-wave units had been destroyed, but the specific orders given and decisions made by individual surviving officers in the period between landing and the eventual assault on the wire are not documented.

choice_echoes.historical_record: LCA craft approaching Dog Green Sector on June 6, 1944, encountered a belt of German beach obstacles beginning approximately 250 yards from the waterline at the time of the assault, which was conducted near low tide. The obstacles included Element C steel gates, Belgian Gates, stakes tipped with Teller mines, and hedgehogs. Many craft were destroyed or holed by these obstacles. The U.S. Army Engineer Special Brigades tasked with clearing sixteen fifty-yard gaps through the obstacle belt were unable to complete their mission due to casualties and the presence of infantry sheltering among the obstacles. Royal Navy after-action reports note that tidal drift and smoke caused significant navigational disorientation among coxswains. No systematic record exists of the specific navigational choices made by individual coxswains at Dog Green.

Elements of the 2nd Ranger Battalion landed at Omaha Beach on June 6, 1944, with Dog Company among the units assigned to the Dog Green and Dog White sector area. The battalion's after-action report records that craft were hit by fire before and during the landing and that men entered the water at varying distances from shore. The shingle bank below the bluffs at Dog Green provided the first significant defilade from direct fire. German MG-42 positions at WN-71, located at the western edge of the Vierville draw, had direct fields of fire across the beach. The 2nd Ranger Battalion's after-action report records that the battalion suffered significant casualties crossing the beach but does not reconstruct individual movements.

The 29th Infantry Division's medical after-action report for June 6, 1944, records that the first-wave landing at Dog Green Sector produced casualties at a rate that overwhelmed available medical personnel immediately. The report notes that many wounded men were unable to move out of the surf and drowned as the tide rose. Aid men who landed with the assault waves faced conditions in which standard triage protocols — prioritizing the salvageable wounded — conflicted with the physical impossibility of moving casualties out of the water under fire. The report does not reconstruct individual triage decisions. Survivor testimony collected by the U.S. Army Center of Military History describes aid men working in the surf under direct fire but does not document specific individual decisions at the level of individual casualties.

The 116th Infantry Regiment's after-action report for June 6, 1944, records that by approximately 0800 hours, surviving elements of multiple companies and attached units had consolidated at the shingle bank below the bluffs in the Dog Green and Dog White sectors. The report notes that the wire and mined obstacles at the base of the Vierville draw represented the primary physical obstacle to advance off the beach in that sector. The 2nd Ranger Battalion's after-action report corroborates the presence of mixed unit groups at the shingle. The specific moment of inter-unit recognition and the communication that established the draw's wire as the shared tactical objective is not reconstructed in either report.

USS McCook (DD-496) action report for June 6, 1944, records that the destroyer engaged targets at Omaha Beach beginning in the early morning hours and continued fire support throughout the day. The report records specific engagements against gun emplacements and troop concentrations on the beach and bluffs. USS Carmick (DD-493) similarly recorded close-range fire support against strongpoints in the Dog sector. The destroyers closed to ranges described in action reports as approximately 800 yards in some instances. The specific intelligence about WN-71's firing arc that enabled effective engagement is not reconstructed in surviving naval records. The Naval Gunfire Support after-action report for Operation Neptune notes that communication between shore fire control parties and ships was disrupted by casualties among liaison personnel in the first wave.

Captain Taylor Fellers, commanding officer of Company A, 116th Infantry Regiment, was killed in the first minutes of the landing at Dog Green Sector on June 6, 1944. The company suffered casualties estimated at over 90 percent in the first thirty minutes. Gordon Harrison's 'Cross-Channel Attack' (U.S. Army in World War II series, 1951) records that the Vierville draw was eventually breached and Exit D-1 opened through the actions of mixed groups of survivors, including Rangers and infantry, acting largely without intact command structures. The report of the Army Board of Review on Omaha Beach operations notes that the destruction of first-wave company command elements meant that tactical decisions in the critical period between 0700 and 0900 hours were made at the squad and individual level rather than at the platoon or company level.
